transcript.text: Hello. This video is What I Think About Shakers. I listened to the Wikipedia article of it today, so I figured I would do the video.
Shakers are a religion in America that was back in the 1800s, 1700s or something like that. I can't remember.

And they were a Christian sect who were called Shakers because they would dance and they would shake and they'd express their faith and their joy in being alive and this connection with God and stuff.
And I think that's cool. I like that. It was a religion that I think there's like four Shakers or eight Shakers in some county in New York or something these days.
But it was a religion that had a lot of female leaders. It was like, I guess, known as like the female-led religion.
Females wrote books and stuff about Shaker religions, so that's a cool thing.
They made really good furniture that lasted really long and inspired functionalist architects of, I guess, when that came around in the 50s or something.
And I guess one of their desks or something was sold for like $100,000 or something recently.
And they invented a whole bunch of things, too.
And I like that.

But they had some—they kind of took that concept, that Christian concept, false Christian concept of, you know, the body being separate from God and a defilement of God and, you know, and so anything having to do with the body is bad.
So anyways, they didn't believe in sex, even if you were married.
And I don't even know if they believed in marriage, but they didn't believe in sex at all.
And so if they wanted to have children, they had to adopt.
And because of that, the religion, you know, I guess they did—there weren't enough kids to adopt back then.
And because of that, the religion died out back in like 1900 or 1910 or something like that.
And like, I guess in church, when the men and women would go to sermon or whatever, the men and women had to sit on separate aisles.
They totally segregated them.
So I don't like that.
That's a little bit—I mean, to each his own, if that's what got them off.
But I don't—I think that's unnecessary.
And it would be unhealthy to bring a kid up in that kind of a situation.
But anyways, I don't know if they're related to the Quakers.
Originally, they were called—I guess it was a derogatory term, Shakers, because the Shake.
But now they're called the Shakers, so I don't know what their original name of their religion was.
But anyways, that's really short, and that's what I think about the Shakers.
